coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of nickel ferrite (NiFe2O4) nano-catalyst films for ciprofloxacin degradation

coredata.dc:description: The challenges associated with efficient removal of ciprofloxacin (CIP), such as low efficiency, high energy consumption, and the generation of harmful by-products have prompted the development of environmentally friendly degradation techniques. This study investigates the synthesis and characterization of nickel ferrite doped with cellulose acetate (NiFe2O4/CA) composite films and coupling with Dielectric Barrier Discharge (DBD) reactor to facilitate ciprofloxacin degradation. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis revealed the presence of spherical NiFe2O4 nanoparticles exhibiting enhanced surface particulate morphology and pore structure following the calcination process. This structural modification resulted in improved adsorption capabilities. The SEM images of NiFe2O4/CA composite films exhibited consistent catalyst performance in a DBD plasma setup across multiple cycles. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) illustrated well-dispersed surfaces with a hollow structure and symmetrical distribution of carbon dots (CDs). Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis confirmed uniform dispersion of atomic components in the nanoparticles. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns indicated high crystallinity and effective integration of NiFe2O4 and CA, supporting their stability for reuse. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis revealed elemental composition and oxidation status, confirming the successful incorporation of carbon dots and metal-oxygen bonds. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) validated the synthesis of NiFe2O4/CA composite films, while Raman spectroscopy further confirmed characteristic peaks, affirming efficient fabrication. The degradation results revealed an upsurge efficiency of 86.13 % for 10 mg/L CIP after 60 min of treatment with 9 % NiFe2O4/CA films, which outperformed other catalysts reported in previous studies. These findings underscore the distinctive performance of incorporating CA into NiFe2O4 NCs and shed light on their potential application for efficient degradation of CIP in the environment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The challenges associated with efficient removal of ciprofloxacin (CIP), such as low efficiency, high energy consumption, and the generation of harmful by-products have prompted the development of environmentally friendly degradation techniques. This study investigates the synthesis and characterization of nickel ferrite doped with cellulose acetate (NiFe2O4/CA) composite films and coupling with Dielectric Barrier Discharge (DBD) reactor to facilitate ciprofloxacin degradation. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis revealed the presence of spherical NiFe2O4 nanoparticles exhibiting enhanced surface particulate morphology and pore structure following the calcination process. This structural modification resulted in improved adsorption capabilities. The SEM images of NiFe2O4/CA composite films exhibited consistent catalyst performance in a DBD plasma setup across multiple cycles. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) illustrated well-dispersed surfaces with a hollow structure and symmetrical distribution of carbon dots (CDs). Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis confirmed uniform dispersion of atomic components in the nanoparticles. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns indicated high crystallinity and effective integration of NiFe2O4 and CA, supporting their stability for reuse. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis revealed elemental composition and oxidation status, confirming the successful incorporation of carbon dots and metal-oxygen bonds. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) validated the synthesis of NiFe2O4/CA composite films, while Raman spectroscopy further confirmed characteristic peaks, affirming efficient fabrication. The degradation results revealed an upsurge efficiency of 86.13 % for 10 mg/L CIP after 60 min of treatment with 9 % NiFe2O4/CA films, which outperformed other catalysts reported in previous studies. These findings underscore the distinctive performance of incorporating CA into NiFe2O4 NCs and shed light on their potential application for efficient degradation of CIP in the environment.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics constantly released into the environment pose serious risks as emerging pollutants owing to toxicity and persistency in aquatic ecosystems [1–3]. These substances are introduced into the environment through ingestion by both humans and poultry. Upon ingestion, they may be excreted either in their original form or as metabolic byproducts through urine, ultimately finding their way into the sewage system [4,5]. One of the most commonly used antibiotics for treating a wide range of infectious diseases, including serious urogenital maladies, ligament and bone infections, and other bacterial illnesses, is ciprofloxacin [6,7]. The widespread use of ciprofloxacin has sparked worries about its effects on the environment and public health, despite the drug's efficacy in treating these illnesses. Within living things, a significant amount of this antibiotic stays unaltered until it is eliminated through urine and released into the environment. Antibiotic resistance is likely to develop as a result of its environmental persistence [8,9], therefore necessitating the efficient removal of ciprofloxacin from contaminated water.

The use of plasma-catalysis has been extensively researched to enhance the removal of different kinds of persistent contaminants, owing to the integration of the advantage of both the extremely specific responses inherent in catalysis, combined with the rapid effects generated by the plasma. Multiple studies have investigated the incorporation of various catalysts into the discharge system, including silica gel and alumina [10], NiO2 and Ag2O [11,12], TiO2 photocatalyst, TiO2/Fe3O4 [13], metal (Fe, Mn and Ce) doped TiO2 [14]. The utilization of plasma-catalyst combined technology offers various benefits, including expansion and dispersion of active species, reduced power usage, enhanced catalyst activation, and extended longevity of active species. The short lifespan of radicals and oxidants from plasma decreases pollutant breakdown rates. However, adding nanomaterials can enhance removal efficiency by aiding pollutant adsorption and extending radical lifespans, thus improving plasma-based removal processes [4].

Nickel ferrites (NiFe2O4) nano-catalysts (NCs) possess an inverted spinel form [15], chemically denoted by
[
F e
3 +
↓
]
A
[
M
2 +
↑
Fe
3 +
↑
]
B
O 2
− 4
, of which M2+ represents a cation with a divalent configuration while the arrows point to opposing spins within the A and B spots. This arrangement depicts a unique magnetic behavior, which is crucial for understanding their catalytic properties in various applications. The configuration consists of ions containing Fe3+ in 8 quadrilateral charged particles as well as Fe3+ and Ni2+ ions occupying 16 octahedral cations [16]. Owing to its distinct characteristics of NiFe2O4, which encompass a large surface domain, magnetic recuperation, and adjustable catalytic activity [17,18], it is used in a broad variety of catalytic reactions, such as oxidation, reduction, and hydrolysis [19–21]. However, the majority of catalysts cause environmental problems and can lead to the development of hazardous intermediates, especially in cases where catalyst recovery is ineffective, and information regarding catalyst recovery is seldom reported [22].

Moreover, ascertaining the composition, geomorphological features, as well as chemical characteristics of NiFe2O4 NCs is made possible by various characterization techniques. To clarify the crystal structure, particle size, surface morphology, and chemical composition of the synthesized nano-catalysts, advanced characterization techniques such as X-ray diffraction (XRD) [23], Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) [24] are employed. Given this, Xian et al. reported on the synthesis of magnetic nickel ferrite catalysts with high stability and magnetic properties for efficient persulfate activation to remove aqueous refractory organics [25].

In this study, we hypothesized that plasma DBD coupled with NiFe2O4 could efficiently remove CIP from wastewater. Based on the preceding, the study focuses on the fabrication and characterization of NiFe2O4 nanoparticles, a composite film made from cellulose acetate doped with NiFe2O4, and a synergistic system incorporating NiFe2O4/CA and DBD plasma for treating CIP in wastewater. In addition, the study aims to understand the property interactions controlling the catalytic behavior (structural, chemical, and physical) of nickel ferrite nano-catalysts, providing insights into the practical utilization of NiFe2O4/CA as an advanced nano-catalysts for wastewater treatment.

The following reagents, specifically powdered activated carbon (PAC), ferric trichloride (FeCl3 .6H2O), nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2 .6H2O), ammonia (NH3 .H2O), ferrous sulfate (FeSO4 .7H2O), formic acid (CH2O2), acetone (C3H6O), and ethanol (C₂H₆O) were obtained from Sinopharm Group Biochemical Reagent Co., Ltd., whereas acetonitrile (C2H3N, HPLC grade) was acquired from Shanghai Maclin Chemical Technological Company. CA and CIP were supplied by Shanghai Genie Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. Additionally, the pure (NiFe2O4) used as a novel activated persulfate catalyst was prepared in the lab. Distilled water was used to prepare the solutions, and all chemical compounds of analytical grade were utilized as received, without any specialized purification.

FeCl3, FeSO4, and Ni(NO3)3 were liquified in 50 mL of distilled water at a predetermined molecular proportion. A precise amount of PAC, which aids in the uniform synthesis of nickel ferrite by acting as a template, dispersant, and stabilizer, enhancing its physical and chemical properties, was then added to the mixture, and the reaction was stirred in a water bath at a specified temperature of 60 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, the pH was adjusted to the range of 9∼10 using NH3·H2O, and the reaction was further stirred in a water bath for an additional hour. At this juncture, the entire reaction was exposed to vacuum filtration to drain the water from the mixture. Following the reaction, the catalyst underwent drying in a 100 °C blast dryer for 1–2 h and calcination at a designated temperature of 700 °C for 4 h in a horse muffle/boiling furnace.

The following section outlines the process involved in fabricating NiFe2O4/CA synthetic composite films with a 3 % NiFe2O4 content as a blueprint: Initially, a conical flask was sequentially filled with 50 mL of acetone and 0.225 g of pure NiFe2O4 NCs. Following that, the mixture was agitated for 4 h at ambient temperature to ensure an even distribution of NiFe2O4 in the acetone. Subsequently, the concoction underwent stirring in a 45 °C water bath for 3 h, during which 7.5 g of CA was incrementally added in three batches. The solution obtained was evenly poured into a 24.08 cm2 square-shaped culture dish, with 5 mL used for each NiFe2O4/CA film preparation. Plastic wrap was then used to seal the dish. After 24 h, a gel sheet was formed, and each sheet was soaked in distilled water to eliminate any residue of NiFe2O4 on the outermost layer. The final step involved obtaining the NiFe2O4/CA films after air drying, which were then stored for subsequent use as shown in Fig. 1 . Utilizing the same procedure, films with varying NiFe2O4 content (5 %, 7 %, 9 %, and 11 %), as well as sole CA films (without NiFe2O4 addition), were also made using the same process.

The microstructure, morphological, and elemental content of various NiFe2O4/CA films were characterized and measured by scanning electron microscopy (Regulus 8100, Hitachi, SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan) coupled with energy dispersive spectroscope (EDS, Japan, SOD2830). X-ray diffraction (XRD, Brooke D2 PHASER) was utilized to investigate the crystal structure. The scanning array was 2θ = 10–80°, and the speed was 5° min−1. Using an X-ray source discharge and Al Kα radiation (hv = 1486.6 eV), an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) was utilized to examine the element's chemical condition. The C, O, Fe, and Ni elemental compositions of the specimens were examined using an elemental analyzer (XPS peak analyzer). The alteration of geomorphological collections within 400–4000 cm−1 was analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, United States, Therma Engineered Nicolet iS20). Eventually, Raman spectrometry (RS United States, Therma Precision, DXR) in an array of 100–3500 cm−1 was used to describe the chemical construct.

A single wastewater treatment experiment was conducted in this study using a 2 L solution containing 10 mg/L of CIP. The entire reaction time was maintained at 60 min, and samples were collected every 10 min for analysis. The concentration of the CIP after-treatment was analyzed using a high-performance liquid chromatography apparatus (HPLC, China, Lunan, LC-1000) assembled with a UV detector and a Waters Symmetry C18 column (Φ4.6 × 250 mm, 5 μm particle size). The mobile phase consisted of a solution containing 13 % acetonitrile and 87 % water (with 0.1 % formic acid) at a volume-to-volume ratio. The solution was delivered at a flow rate of 1 ml min−1. The volume of the injection was 20 μL and the wavelength used for detection was set to 277 nm. The degrading efficiency (η, %) of the CIP was determined using Eq. (1):
(1)

where η (%) represents CIP degradation efficiency, C

(%ΔE) represents a percentage increase in efficiency, (Y) is the DBD system with 9 % NiFe2O4/CA catalyst efficiency, and (X) is the DBD solo system efficiency.

The CIP concentration in the aqueous solution was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600i Shimadzu, China) at a wavelength of 200–600 nm. The spectrophotometer was employed to scan the CIP solution both initially and during the degradation process, allowing the degradation to be monitored by observing the characteristic peaks. All experiments were conducted in triplicate, with standard errors calculated. The adequacy of the data was statistically validated using a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA).

Morphological analysis plays a significant role in the fabrication and characterization of NiFe2O4 nano-catalyst films used for ciprofloxacin degradation. It provides important information about the surface topology, particle size distribution, and structural properties of the catalyst. Therefore, we have used advanced techniques such as scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) to examine the nanoscale structure of catalyst films. These techniques allow us to determine the arrangement of nanoparticles, the level of porosity, and the roughness of the film's surface. The following section describes their results in detail.

SEM analysis was utilized to examine the surface morphology and microstructure of NiFe2O4 nano-catalyst films that were synthesized for ciprofloxacin degradation. The morphology of the outer surfaces of the synthetic composite films and the pure catalyst as seen by SEM is presented in Fig. 2 . The observed morphology of the NiFe2O4 NPs for the pure NiFe2O4 sample Fig. 2(a) is spherical, with homogenous dimensions and an evenly distributed aggregation propensity. This morphology can be ascribed to the magnetic characteristics of the material. The similarity between this morphology and the findings reported in the available research confirms the conclusions drawn from the XPS as well as XRD analysis [26–29]. Besides, the calcination process involving Fe and Ni elements resulted in a notable augmentation of surface particulate matter, coupled with the development of a substantial pore structure. The formation of these orifice structures contributes to the establishment of more favorable conditions for effective interaction between the catalyst and the oxidant, enhancing the adsorption performance of the synthetic amalgam films. This highlights the positive impact of the calcination process on catalyst efficiency. Regarding the CA films Fig. 2(b), the presence of CA particles on the surface was evident. Moreover, when 3 % NiFe2O4 was merged into the synthetic composite films, NiFe2O4 particles were also observed. As the proportion of NiFe2O4 increased further, accumulation of the NiFe2O4 particles occurred. With regards to the SEM image of the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA films, a negligible quantity of CA particles could still be discernible. Besides, Fig. 2(e and f) exhibited apparent agglomeration rather than even distribution which could be due to the intrinsic properties and high surface energy of the NiFe2O4 nanoparticles, which tend to aggregate to minimize their surface energy [30,31]. Similarly, Nadeem et al. (2024) reported that SEM images of nickel ferrites revealed ultra-small grains and particle agglomeration, with particle size increasing and distribution becoming more homogeneous upon annealing, further showing the annealed sample with agglomerated particles having a granularity of 10 μm [32]. Conversely, in the 11 % NiFe2O4/CA films, the NiFe2O4 particles completely covered the CA particles, indicating a potential excessive load of NiFe2O4. Moreover, the SEM analysis verified the repeatability and durability of the NiFe2O4/CA synthetic composite films within the DBD plasma setup. Fig. 2(h) provides visual evidence that the NiFe2O4/CA synthetic composite films retained their catalytic effect even after the fourth cycle experiment. SEM and XRD images of the NiFe2O4/CA synthetic composite films were obtained before and after four cycles of utilization to elucidate the impact of DBD on the films, as depicted in Fig. 2(f and h) and 5(b) respectively. Notably, SEM images of the NiFe2O4/CA films after four rounds of recycling showed a minor agglomeration phenomenon.

TEM analysis is utilized to explore the ultrastructure and crystalline properties of NiFe2O4 nano-catalyst, while also unveiling the morphologies of the CDs. Fig. 3 illustrates the TEM images of the catalyst captured at different magnifications. The figure depicts an evenly distributed and dispersed surface of the catalyst, along with a hollow structure in the interior. Furthermore, the nickel ferrite photocatalyst's (NFO) surface symmetrical distribution of carbon dots (CDs) is evident by the TEM imagery. The (001) and (311) crystal planes of CDs and NFO, respectively, are represented by the two lattice spacings (0.21 and 0.25 nm) found in the CDs/NFO-0.5 composite [33]. This empirical evidence validates the effective integration of CDs into the CDs/NFO hybrid system.

EDS, a robust analytical method is used to examine the elemental composition and distribution inside NiFe2O4 nano-catalyst films designed for the degradation of ciprofloxacin. To determine the elemental composition and distribution of the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA film sample, an EDS line scan and mapping analysis of the outermost layer of the catalyst was conducted and the findings are presented in Fig. 4 (a). Table 1 displays the weight and atomic ratios of each element, displaying the elemental composition of the analyzed sample in terms of both weight percentage and atom percentage. The graphics illustrate that the catalyst surface comprises four elements: carbon (C), oxygen (O), iron (Fe), and nickel (Ni). In terms of weight percentage, the sample comprises 61.09 % carbon, 22.97 % oxygen, 13.21 % iron, and 2.73 % nickel. On the other hand, when atom percentage is taken into account, the composition is 74.74 % carbon, 21.10 % oxygen, 3.48 % iron, and 0.68 % nickel. These values sum up to a complete value of 100.00 % as well as indicate the relative abundance of each element in the sample, providing insights into its chemical makeup. The higher atom percentage of carbon suggests a greater prevalence of carbon atoms in the sample at the atomic level, while the weight percentage emphasizes the contribution of each element to the overall mass of the material.

The morphological analysis using EDS on the sample presented in Fig. 4 indicates the existence of entire constituents of the NiFe2O4 molecular component. This observation aligns with the conclusions deduced from the XPS examination. Fig. 4(b) provides an overview image showing the layered structure of all four elements, while Fig. 4(c–f) displays the elemental composition of each discrete element of C, O, Fe, and Ni. Additionally, the EDS cartographic images shown in the figure demonstrate the uniform dispersion of all atomic components within the nanoparticles. Notably, there is no region devoid of any atoms, confirming the efficacy of the preparation method employed for the materials.

XRD analysis aims to confirm the crystal structure and phase purity of the synthesized NiFe2O4 nano-catalyst films, ensuring their suitability for ciprofloxacin degradation. Fig. 5 (a and b) depict the XRD patterns of the pure NiFe2O4 molecular component, CA films, and 9 % NiFe2O4/CA films before and after utilization. XRD analysis was initially conducted on the pure NiFe2O4 sample to ascertain its chemical purity and crystallographic structure. The NiFe2O4 sample depicted in Fig. 6 (a) revealed distinct and intense diffraction peaks, indicative of its high crystalline nature. The observed peaks at 2θ value corresponding to 18.4° (111), 30.1° (220), 35.7° (311), 37.3° (222), 43.4° (400), 53.8° (422), 57.4° (511), 62.9° (440), 71.5° (620), 74.6° (533), 75.6° (622) and 79.6° (444) [34] align precisely with the characteristic peaks documented in the XRD standard card (86–2267). This alignment, along with the spinel cubic structure of NiFe2O4 (JCPDF No.10–0325) indexing, supports the conclusion that the active substance formed on the catalyst surface is nickel ferrite. The distinct pinnacles of the CA films emerged around 2θ = 18° [35]. Upon examination of the XRD configuration of the NiFe2O4/CA films, the distinctive pinnacles observed in both NiFe2O4 and CA were untiring. However, their intensities exhibited a decrease maintaining a constant pattern, suggesting the effective integration of NiFe2O4 and CA. These outcomes align with the deductions originating in the Raman Spectral analysis. As depicted in the XRD examination presented in Fig. 5(b), it is evident that the pinnacles associated with both NiFe2O4 and CA persist in those of NiFe2O4/CA films, regardless of their use. However, NiFe2O4 peak intensity levels remained consistently stable, while the intensity of CA exhibited an initial increase. This phenomenon may be attributed to the fractional segregation of NiFe2O4 departed from the NiFe2O4/CA films during the emission phase, consequently influencing their catalytic performance. Given this, it is plausible to infer that the produced NiFe2O4/CA synthetic composite films exhibited reasonable stability, enabling their reuse.

The valence of components within the synthetic composite films was examined using XPS to better assess the surface elemental and chemical makeup as well as the oxidation status of the NiFe2O4 products. The 9 % NiFe2O4/CA synthetic composite films consist of C, O, Fe, and Ni, as visible in the comprehensive spectra displayed in Fig. 6(a). As a result, the elemental peaks of the C1s, O1s, Fe, and Ni were discernible.Fig. 6(b–e) illustrates the C1s, O1s, Fe 2p, and Ni 2p spectra, respectively. The C 1s spectrum shown in Fig. 6(b) for the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA synthetic composite unveils a total of three distinct peaks, with respective energies of 284.8, 286.1, and 287.5 eV. These pinnacles correspond with the C-C/C=C, C-O, followed by C=O bonds, indicating the effective incorporation of CDs in the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA synthetic composite system. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s, illustrated in Fig. 6(c), exhibits distinct pinnacles at binding energies of 531.5 and 533.6 eV. These peaks are attributed to metal-oxygen bonds (Ni-O/Fe-O), which are known to be typical M − O bonds, as well as adsorbed water (O-H), which could also be associated with the physical or chemical retention of H2O on the outermost layer, respectively [36,37]. A detailed spectrum of Fe2p in Fig. 6(d) shows two principal peaks centered at 713.5 and 726.5 eV, conforming to the Fe2p3/2 and Fe2p1/2 spin-orbit peaks, respectively [38]. These peaks signify the Fe(III) oxidation condition present in the NiFe2O4 products. Besides, the peak at 717.9 eV is ascribed to satellite peaks [39]. The Ni 2p core-level spectrum obtained from fitting Fig. 6(e) comprises two shake-up satellites and two spin-orbit doublets. The two satellite peaks that are initially identified as indicating shake-up-type features of nickel are approximately positioned at 863.9 and 881.3 eV [40]. According to Ref. [41], the emergence of these peaks has led scientists to thoroughly examine the chemical state. On that note, the spin-orbit peaks of Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, respectively, are represented by two notable peaks at the binding energies of 858.2 and 876.3 eV, suggesting the existence of Ni2+ and Ni3+ [42]. Ni seems to be in a 2+ oxidation state based on the measured energy difference of 18.1 eV [43].

The chemical composition of the NiFe2O4/CA composite film was analyzed by FT-IR detection, providing a complete understanding of its constitution. The FT-IR spectra as seen in Fig. 7 (a) show the distinctive features of the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA films and pure NiFe2O4 NPs. Notably, the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA films exhibited the characteristic peaks at 3475 cm−1 (O − H), 2942 cm−1 (C − H), 1730 cm−1 (C = O), 1369 cm−1 (C − CH3), 1213 cm−1 (C − O − C), and 1029.61 cm−1 (C − O − C) [44]. Moreover, the peak detected at 902.69 cm−1 could be attributed to the saccharide compound from the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA treatment. According to Ref. [45], the tensile vibration of Fe2O3 could be assigned to the noticeable peak at 601 cm−1, whereas the stretching vibration of nickel-oxygen bonds would be linked to the vibration bands at 411 cm−1. Owing to the limitations of our FTIR device, ν2 (400–385 cm−1), ν3 (328 cm−1), and ν4 (below 300 cm−1) were not detected around 400 cm−1. An extra vibration band was found at 1636 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of the pure magnetic nickel-iron oxide (NiFe2O4) catalyst. This vibration band is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of the O–H group in water molecules. Particularly, the stretching vibrations of the iron-oxygen bonds are assigned to the vibration bands between 606 and 1056 cm−1 [46]. According to Ref. [47], two characteristic bands at 606 and 411 cm−1 suggest the NiFe2O4 spinel structure. These bands indicate stretching oxygen–metal vibrations at tetrahedral (O–Fe–O) and octahedral sites (O–Fe–O and O–Ni–O), accordingly. The effective production of the NiFe2O4/CA synthetic composite films is further confirmed by the FT-IR spectra.

Raman spectroscopy, a rapid and user-friendly technology, is invaluable for analyzing chemical bonding, investigating local disorders, and providing crucial insights into material composition and structural characteristics [48,49]. Distinctive characteristics were identified upon examination of the Raman Spectra shown in Fig. 7(b), which included a multitude of substrates, encompassing the CA films, the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA films, and the pure NiFe2O4 NPs. Regarding CA films, distinct pinnacles were detected at 2939 cm−1 and 1129 cm−1, corresponding to the C–H and C–O extension oscillation of glycosidic bonds, sequentially [50]. Besides, the extension oscillation of the C = O, C − O, the flexural oscillations of the H − C − C, as well as C − H were found to correspond with pinnacles at 1742 cm−1, 1258 cm−1, 1368 cm−1, and 1431 cm−1 accordingly [50–52]. In addition, the symmetrical extension oscillation of the C − O − C, extension oscillation of the C–C as well as C–O was associated with the pinnacles at 911 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1, respectively, and the flexural oscillations of
C x
−
H y
remained the source of the pinnacles at 654.1 cm−1 [53]. The NiFe2O4/CA films' RS showed a considerable drop in pinnacle count when compared to the CA films as well as a notable reduction in the pinnacle magnitudes at 2939 cm−1 that was related to the CA films.

Besides, as depicted in the Raman spectra figure, all materials (excluding pure NiFe2O4) unveiled two distinct peaks attributed to carbon materials, identified as D and G bands. The D pinnacle is associated with lattice deficiencies, while the G pinnacle arises from the stretching of entire duos of sp
2 atomic units in rings and chains [54,55]. Specifically, for 9 % NiFe2O4/CA, RS pinnacles of D and G-bands tend to be roughly sited at 1342 cm−1 and 1591 cm−1, accordingly. The analysis above indicates that the amalgamation of CA and NiFe2O4 has an impact on the intensities of characteristic peaks associated with CA and the positioning of the typical peak for NiFe2O4/CA. The occurrence of all identified peaks in the RS of the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA composite serves as confirmation of the efficient fabrication of the composite material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of catalyst percentages for CIP degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The optimization of catalyst percentages is crucial in the pursuit of enhancing the degradation efficiency of CIP. This study investigates the production and combination of NiFe2O4/CA catalysts at five different proportions (3 %, 5 %, 7 %, 9 %, and 11 %), as shown in Fig. 1. Our goal is to determine the catalyst proportion that results in the best efficiency in CIP degradation. The findings, illustrated in Fig. 8 , showcase the degradation rates of CIP within a synergetic setup consisting of DBD plasma coupled with varying additions of NiFe2O4/CA (3 %, 5 %, 7 %, 9 %, and 11 %). These percentages were found to have higher CIP degradation efficiencies in comparison to the DBD solo system. The degradation efficiencies of the different proportions, 3 %, 5 %, 7 %, 9 %, and 11 % were 69.33 %, 76.33 %, 80.66 %, 86.13 %, and 76.66 respectively. Interestingly, the DBD system, which possessed a 9 % NiFe2O4/CA addition, attained a significantly higher degradation percentage of 86.13 % after a 60-min treatment. DBD solo system exhibited a lower degradation percentage of CIP, amounting to 60.66 %. This result represents a percentage increase in efficiency of 41.98 % of CIP in comparison to the degradation observed in the DBD solo system and that of the DBD system coupled with the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA.

Moreover, the aforementioned findings provide additional evidence that the film with a higher concentration of NiFe2O4 NPs (11 %) is less efficient than the film with a 9 % concentration, primarily due to increased NP aggregation at higher concentrations. While the SEM image of the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA film in Fig. 2(f) shows some agglomeration of NiFe2O4, it still exhibited higher catalytic efficiency compared to the 11 % NiFe2O4/CA film. This could be attributed to the uneven dispersion of NiFe2O4 within the composite films, where a lower ratio ensures better distribution, while a higher ratio leads to excessive agglomeration, reducing the contact surface area between active sites and the target pollutant [35]. As the NP concentration rises, the particles tend to cluster together, disrupting uniformity and creating regions with varying properties, which reduces overall efficiency. The film may have also surpassed its percolation threshold, where an optimal NP concentration enhances the film's properties, but excess leads to diminished returns and defects. Additionally, increased NiFe2O4 content darkened the composite films, as shown in Fig. 2(g). This color change could potentially affect the interactions between plasma-generated reactive species and the NiFe2O4 surface, reducing the overall effectiveness of the DBD plasma treatment in degrading CIP. Therefore, while the 9 % concentration allows better NP dispersion and integration, enhancing efficiency and catalytic properties, the 11 % concentration introduces inefficiencies due to these negative effects. Hence, this optimal balance in the 9 % composite allows for more efficient utilization of the catalytic properties of NiFe2O4, resulting in higher degradation rates of CIP.

The UV–Vis spectra of the original CIP solution and the solutions after 60 min of treatment in the DBD plasma system, with and without the inclusion of 9 % NiFe2O4/CA, were recorded. The results, covering the range of 200–600 nm, shows a decrease in the intensities of the characteristic peaks in the spectra after 60 min of reaction in both the DBD alone and the DBD systems with NiFe2O4/CA composite films (Fig. 9 ). This indicates that CIP degradation occurred. Additionally, the DBD plasma system coupled with 9 % NiFe2O4/CA exhibited significantly lower peak intensities, demonstrating that the composite films effectively promote the breakdown of CIP through catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Durability of the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA composite films
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the durability and repeatability of the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA composite films in the DBD plasma system, four cycle tests were conducted on the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA films. Following a single use, the films were removed from the reactor, rinsed with deionized water, and left to dry in the air for the subsequent cycle test. Fig. 10 displays the experimental findings. The figure demonstrates that the NiFe2O4/CA composite films retained their catalytic effects during the fourth cycle testing. After undergoing four cycles of use, the degradation percentage of the CIP solution exhibited a drop from 86.13 % to 84.65 %. SEM and XRD images of the NiFe2O4/CA composite films were obtained before and after four cycles of usage to analyze the impact of the DBD on the films. The results are presented in Fig. 2(f and h) and Fig. 5(b), respectively. The SEM images of the NiFe2O4/CA films, taken after four cycles of recycling, revealed a minor agglomeration phenomenon. In Fig. 5(b), the XRD analysis revealed that the peaks corresponding to NiFe2O4 and CA were still present in the NiFe2O4/CA films after usage. However, the peak intensities of NiFe2O4 were more dominant and higher compared to the peak intensities belonging to CA. This could be due to slight structural changes, surface fouling, or minor loss of active sites on the NiFe2O4 composite films, leading to a small but noticeable drop in the overall degradation performance after repeated use. Based on the results, it can be inferred that the NiFe2O4/CA composite films that were made exhibited satisfactory stability and were capable of being reused.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the optimized NiFe2O4/CA catalyst percentage in comparison with other studies for CIP degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The assessment of the efficacy of the optimized NiFe2O4/CA catalyst in degrading CIP pollutants is essential for evaluating its performance. The objective of this study is to compare its performance with previous research findings, providing insight into its potential as a catalyst for CIP degradation. By comparing the catalytic activity of NiFe2O4/CA with that reported in other studies, we aim to elucidate its effectiveness and explore avenues for further enhancement in the treatment of CIP-contaminated wastewater. Given this, Table 2 summarizes the performance of the optimized catalyst percentage (9 % NiFe2O4/CA) in comparison with several catalysts from previously reported studies for CIP degradation. Notably, NiFe2O4/CA distinguished itself from the other catalysts by taking into consideration its unique benefit. One of its key characteristics was the NiFe2O4/CA catalyst's incredible ability to accelerate the degradation process. Comparing NiFe2O4/CA to other catalysts, the degradation duration was noticeably shorter. Given this, NiFe2O4/CA has higher catalytic activity, which speeds up the breakdown of CIP molecules. Additionally, the study revealed that NiFe2O4/CA showed improved efficiency while utilizing a small dose of catalyst. This characteristic not only emphasizes the catalytic capabilities of NiFe2O4/CA but equally suggests a cost-effective and resource-conserving method of using it to accelerate the degradation of CIP, indicating its potential as a highly effective and resource-efficient catalyst in environmental remediation processes. Ultimately, it is obvious from the presented findings that the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA catalysts were effective CIP activators. The degrading efficiency of CIP by the magnetized nanocomposite catalysts (NiFe2O4/CA) was evaluated with previously reported catalysts. In particular, the 9 % NiFe2O4/CA catalysts demonstrated an advantage over Blast furnace dust/catalytic-ceramic-filler, Fe–N@MC500, and Zn-doped Cu2O. This advantage lies in the considerably shorter degradation time and the requirement of only a small treatment volume of water to achieve a comparable degradation effect [25].

The study demonstrates the effectiveness of incorporating cellulose acetate with nickel ferrite nanoparticles (NiFe2O4/CA) for CIP degradation in a DBD plasma setup. Morphological analyses confirm the successful fabrication of NiFe2O4/CA composite films with enhanced surface particulate morphology and pore structure, leading to improved adsorption capabilities. Characterization techniques such as SEM, TEM, EDS, XRD, XPS, FT-IR, and Raman spectroscopy provide detailed insights into the structural, chemical, and physical properties of the catalyst. The optimized catalyst, 9 % NiFe2O4/CA, demonstrates a significant enhancement in CIP degradation efficiency of 86.13 % for 10 mg/L CIP after 60 min of treatment, which outperformed other catalysts reported in previous studies. This study emphasizes the potential of NiFe2O4/CA composite films as advanced nano-catalysts for efficient degradation of CIP in wastewater treatment, offering a promising solution to mitigate the environmental impact of antibiotic pollutants.
